## [00:00 - 03:03]

Laying out your lessons. Welcome back. You have done so much work in this program and it is all starting to pay off. And I hope you see how the work you did in previous sections is getting pulled into your course creation. In this lesson, I'm going to walk you through the mastermind approach to creating your lessons for your course. Here's how it'll go. I'm going to teach you the structure. We're going to do one sample lesson together and then you can lay out your lesson in your workbook. Again, we'll be doing just one lesson together so you'll repeat the same process for each lesson in your course. If you have six lessons based on your six teaching topics, we'll go through one lesson together and then you might want to go back and re-watch this video to walk through each of your additional lessons. That's what's great about on-demand education. It's so convenient for your students to stop, start and re-watch whenever they like. So grab your workbook and let's dive in. Remember how I said to start any course by determining your high-level outcome? We need to do the same thing for each lesson we create with a little bit of a twist. Before creating any lesson, I want you to ask, what do I want my students to have done by the end of this lesson? In other words, what is your goal for that lesson? For example, for this particular lesson, my goal is that you will understand how to effectively structure a lesson and to have laid out your first lesson. With that in mind, I then can go on laying out the content that fits that goal. And we have a great framework for you to use when laying out your lesson content. We've discovered that the most effective teaching structure is one where we go from big picture to detail. This process allows people to create space in their brain for what you're teaching. If you just jump right into the details without showing people how everything fits together first, it can be really confusing and it makes it difficult for people to follow along. That's why we will start by talking about our philosophy. It might seem strange to talk about the philosophy of something like wardrobes or coffee, but it is critical. You want to tell your students why this topic is important. Why do people need to know about it? What are some of your beliefs around this topic? If you go back to our very first lesson together, you'll see that I started off by telling you why I love courses, why they are so powerful and why they should be a part of every business. That's my philosophy for courses. Hopefully, that began the process of you making room in your brain for the future lessons. I'll give you some more examples of this. I'm not talking about a long dissertation when I say philosophy. It can just be a sentence or two to introduce the topic. After we set the stage with philosophy, we look at context. We provide a high level 30,000 foot view. It's like learning to navigate around a new city. It can help if you see the bird's eye view and get an idea of how things relate to each other. Here's an example. My son and I spent a week in Barcelona a few years ago. One of the very first things I did before we even booked a hotel was to pull up a map

So then you can just relax and follow along because you're in good hands. The next element we include in our structure is examples, which are critical. Some people are really great at abstract thinking, but most of us need examples for something to become real and understandable. So to recap, our recommended approach to content creation is to set our goal for the individual lesson, which should be related to your outcome for the course, then go from big picture to detail. We start with why people need to know or care about something, the philosophy. Then we talk about the context, how what they're learning fits into what they already know. So let's start with her goal for this first lesson, wardrobe assessment.
